However, the computationally intensive nature of the manual and heuris- tic sequential forward process poses a significant challenge, impeding the real-time adoption of these models. 1, it can be observed that none of the aforementioned approaches consider the state of the sampling process, which is contrary to common sense. To overcome this, we propose the Reinforced Active Sampling Scheduler, termed RaSS, intending to find the op- timal sampling trajectory by actively planning and adjust- ing the sampling steps for each sampling process in time. Extensive experiments on different benchmarks have shown that our RaSS can consistently improve the generation quality and efficiency across various tasks, without introducing signifi- cant computational overhead.

1. Introduction
In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in Dif-
fusion models [14, 23, 44, 62, 64, 66] due to their im-
pressive image-generation capabilities, establishing them as
extremely reliable probabilistic generative models. These
models have effectively mitigated the issues of mode col-
lapse and instability that plagued previous implicit gen-
erative models. Diffusion models have been successfully
applied to a wide range of applications, including text-to-
image generation [21, 53, 67], image editing [10, 28, 47],

computationally intensive nature of the manual and heuris-
tic sequential forward process poses a significant challenge,
impeding the real-time adoption of these models.
Consequently, extensive research has been conducted
to accelerate the repetitive sequential inference speed of
DDPM models, which can be broadly categorized into two
approaches: optimizing sampling efficiency and optimiz-
ing inference efficiency. The optimization of inference ef-
ficiency [29, 45, 46, 50, 59, 73] aims

Abstract Recent years have witnessed the great success of denois- ing diffusion samplers in improving the generative capa- bility and sampling efficiency given a pre-trained diffusion model.
Additionally, it is important to note that RaSS, as a plug-and-play scheduler, is designed to enhance the capa- bilities of baseline samplers rather than replace more ad- vanced samplers.
Additionally, it showcases its versatility by optimizing widely used samplers like DDIM, DPM-solver, and DPM-solver++.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Recent years have witnessed the great success of denois-
ing diffusion samplers in improving the generative capa-
bility and sampling efficiency given a pre-trained diffusion
model. However, most sampling schedulers in diffusion
models lack the sampling dynamics and planning capabil-
ity for future generation results, leading to suboptimal so-
lutions. To overcome this, we propose the Reinforced Active
Sampling Scheduler, termed RaSS, intending to find the op-
timal sampling trajectory by actively planning and adjust-
ing the sampling steps for each sampling process in time.
Concretely, RaSS divides the whole sampling process into
five stages and introduces a reinforcement learning (RL)
agent to continuously monitor the generated instance and
perceive the potential generation results, thereby achieving
optimal instance- and state-adaptive sampling steps deci-
sion. Meanwhile, a sampling reward is designed to assist
the planning capability of the RL agent by balancing the
sampling efficiency and generation quality. The RaSS is a
plug-and-play module, which is applicable to multiple de-
noising diffusion samplers of diffusion models. Extensive
experiments on different benchmarks have shown that our
RaSS can consistently improve the generation quality and
efficiency across various tasks, without introducing signifi-
cant computational overhead.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in Dif-
fusion models [14, 23, 44, 62, 64, 66] due to their im-
pressive image-generation capabilities, establishing them as
extremely reliable probabilistic generative models. These
models have effectively mitigated the issues of mode col-
lapse and instability that plagued previous implicit gen-
erative models. Diffusion models have been successfully
applied to a wide range of applications, including text-to-
image generation [21, 53, 67], image editing [10, 28, 47],
†Corresponding Author.
∗Co-corresponding Author.
image-to-image translation [12, 76], video synthesis [7, 8]
and even image restoration [36, 54, 56, 58]. However, the
computationally intensive nature of the manual and heuris-
tic sequential forward process poses a significant challenge,
impeding the real-time adoption of these models.
Consequently, extensive research has been conducted
to accelerate the repetitive sequential inference speed of
DDPM models, which can be broadly categorized into two
approaches: optimizing sampling efficiency and optimiz-
ing inference efficiency. The optimization of inference ef-
ficiency [29, 45, 46, 50, 59, 73] aims to reduce the single
step inference time of the model, while the optimization of
sampling [11, 18, 30, 40, 65, 78] efficiency aims to reduce
the total number of samples required. Although these meth-
ods have shown improvements in the generation speed of
diffusion models, they have also resulted in a degradation
of model performance. We have identified that due to the
unique long-sequence generation process of diffusion mod-
els, the final quality is not solely determined by the results
of individual inference steps but is also influenced by the
temporal sequence of model samples.
Our goal is to optimize the sampling sequence in diffu-
sion models to accelerate sampling efficiency while enhanc-
ing sample quality. Previous approaches focusing on sam-
pling schedulers have utilized dynamic programming [70]
methods to minimize the model’s evidence lower bound
(ELBO) in order to obtain an optimized K-step schedule.
Another study [69] involves differentiating through sam-
ple quality scores to optimize the sampler. However, these
methods have either failed to improve the quality of gener-
ated samples, as measured by metrics such as Fr ´echet In-
ception Distance (FID), or have imposed limitations on the
applicable task scenarios and data types. Zhang et al.[75]
introduced the concept of reinforcement learning to train
a prompt-specific step scheduler. However, their optimiza-
tion of the sampling sequence is limited, and the approach is
primarily applicable to text-to-image tasks, providing only
marginal improvements compared to the baseline sampler.
Additionally, as shown in fig. 1, it can be observed that none
of the aforementioned approaches consider the state of the
sampling process, which is contrary to common sense.
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
12923

Figure 1. Comparison of various time sequences in diffusion sampling, where orange and blue boxes indicate the sampling pointing and
sampling scheduler respectively. P , I, τ represent the prompt, image, and time point, respectively; stτ represents timesteps at time τ.
To this end, we focus on a challenging topic: how to
devise a stable optimization methodology for the sampling
scheduler of diffusion models that is adaptable across vari-
ous samplers and tasks. To accomplish this, we take into
account the intrinsic characteristics of different sampling
sequences and their impact on the final image quality. We
have observed that different generation objectives and states
have varying requirements for sampling, and perceiving
task instance states can significantly enhance the potential
of baseline samplers, as depicted in the provided figure 3.
By exploiting the temporal dynamics inherent in dif-
fusion sequence sampling, we partition the sampling pro-
cedure into multiple sub-stages. Within each sub-stage,
we employ reinforcement learning (RL) agents to obtain a
sampling scheduler, facilitating continuous monitoring of
instance states. Concurrently, through the incorporation
of a long-term perception mechanism (LTP), the sampling
scheduler is endowed with the capability to discern the la-
tent generative quality of the model, thereby ensuring op-
timization towards global optimality. To bolster the sam-
pling scheduler effectiveness in regulating both the quality
and efficiency of diffusion model generation, we propose
a loss-aware evaluator reward, which adeptly manages the
efficiency of model generation while upholding the requi-
site standard of generated output quality. It is noteworthy
that the sampling scheduler remains lightweight, thereby
circumventing substantial computational overhead.
The contributions of this paper are summarized as fol-
lows:
• We introduce a simple yet effective method for optimiz-
ing the sampling scheduler, which actively plans and ad-
justs the sampling steps for each sampling process in
time, based on the perceptual characteristics of specific
task instance states, without excessive additional compu-
tations.
• RaSS introduces a long-term perception mechanism
(LTP), ensuring the global optimality of sampling sched-
uler decisions. Furthermore, we introduce a loss-aware
evaluation reward to assist the planning capability of the
scheduler by balancing the sampling efficiency and gen-
eration quality.
• RaSS has been validated across multiple datasets, in-
cluding CIFAR10, LSUN-Bedroom, ImageNet, and
COCO2017, covering a range of tasks such as uncon-
ditional image generation, conditional image generation,
and text-to-image generation. Additionally, it showcases
its versatility by optimizing widely used samplers like
DDIM, DPM-solver, and DPM-solver++.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
In contrast to focus on optimizing individual sampling
steps, we present a time schedule optimization framework
for SDE/ODE samplers from a global sampling perspec-
tive. Section 3.1 demonstrated the existence of optimal
sub-sequences within the ODE sampling process through
empirical visualization validation and provided the theoret-
ical basis for reinforcement learning to achieve optimal re-
12925

sults. Section 3.2 utilizes a long-term perceptual mecha-
nism (LTP) to train the sampling scheduler. Finally, Section
3.3 introduces a loss-aware evaluation reward to effectively
balance the baseline sampler’s speed and generation quality
requirements.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce a novel paradigm, RaSS, aimed
at enhancing the performance of baseline samplers. Build-
ing upon the conclusion that perceiving specific task in-
stance states can significantly enhance the quality of the fi-
nal generated images, RaSS divides the training task into
multiple sub-stages and integrates a long-term perception
mechanism (LTP) within each sub-stage to ensure the global
optimality of sampling scheduler decisions, thereby provid-
ing tailored sampling schedule for each task instance. Ex-
tensive experimentation demonstrates the stability and ap-
plicability of the proposed algorithm across various tasks
and samplers, thus making a significant contribution to this
field. Additionally, it is important to note that RaSS, as a
plug-and-play scheduler, is designed to enhance the capa-
bilities of baseline samplers rather than replace more ad-
vanced samplers.
